context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Multi-level Planning and Scale

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of power and legitimacy in multi-level planning? Is the State a site or an actor in multi-level planning? What is the role of resources in multi-level planning? How do collaboration and resistance happen in multi-level planning efforts?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on multi-level planning, and I’m kind of stuck on the main argument. I need help figuring out how to answer the questions about power, legitimacy, whether the state is a site or an actor, and how resources, collaboration, and resistance fit together.
exchanges.tutor:
Good, let's dig in. Before we think about structure, let's make sure the ideas are clear. 

Start with this: in your own words, what do you think 'multi-level planning' means, and why would power and legitimacy even matter in that context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of their reflection and needs to engage with the core concepts before I can help them structure an argument. Rather than scaffolding the whole paper, I should use the Socratic method to get them thinking about the foundational concept first — power and legitimacy in multi-level planning — which will naturally lead into the other questions. I'll ask a focused opening question to activate their prior thinking.

exchanges.student: I think multi-level planning means planning that happens across different scales, like local, regional, national, and maybe even international actors too. Power and legitimacy matter because those actors don’t have equal authority, so some can make rules or control decisions, while others have to justify why they should be listened to or obeyed.
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you've identified something important: unequal authority across scales, and legitimacy as a justification for power. That's a strong foundation.

Now, the prompt asks whether the state is a *site* or an *actor* in multi-level planning. What do you think the difference between those two things would be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a solid foundational answer — they understand scale and have grasped the unequal distribution of authority and the role of legitimacy in justifying power. I should affirm this briefly and push them toward the next key question: is the state a site or an actor? This is a conceptually rich question that requires them to think more carefully, and it connects directly to the prompt. I'll nudge them toward it with a Socratic question.

exchanges.student: I think an actor means the state actually does things, like sets policy, allocates resources, or enforces rules. A site sounds more like the state is the arena where different interests, institutions, and power struggles meet, so maybe it’s not just one unified thing. I’m not totally sure if the assignment wants me to choose one or say it’s both.
exchanges.tutor: You've got the distinction right. Now here's the harder question: can you think of a real-world example — from your readings or from what you know — where the state seems to act as *both* a site and an actor at the same time? What would that tell you about how to answer the prompt?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done well to distinguish between site and actor, and is now wondering whether to choose one or argue both. Rather than resolving this for them, I should push them to think about what evidence or reasoning from the course context would support one position or the other — or both. This keeps them doing the intellectual work and moves toward a stronger argument.

exchanges.student: Maybe decentralization is a good example? The national state acts as an actor when it creates decentralization laws and decides what powers local governments get, but it’s also a site because ministries, local elites, donors, and community groups all fight over what decentralization really means in practice. So I guess that would let me argue the state is both, not just one.
exchanges.tutor:
Excellent — decentralization is a strong example and your reasoning holds up well. That 'both/and' argument is more sophisticated than picking one side.

Now connect this to resources: in your decentralization example, how do you think the distribution of resources shapes who actually has power at each level?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong conceptual leap using decentralization as an example — this is exactly the kind of grounded reasoning the reflection needs. I should affirm this and now push them toward the next part of the prompt: the role of resources. This will help them build a more complete argument and connect the concepts together.

exchanges.student: I think resources kind of determine whether authority is real or just formal. Like a local government might officially have power after decentralization, but if the national level keeps the money, staff, or technical capacity, then the local level can’t really act much. So power depends not just on legal authority but on who controls funding and implementation.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a sharp observation — the gap between formal authority and actual capacity is crucial. Hold onto that.

Now, given everything you've said so far, what do you think motivates actors at different levels to either *collaborate* or *resist* in multi-level planning efforts?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent point about the gap between formal and real authority, which is a key insight for this assignment. They're building a coherent argument. Now I should push them toward the last part of the prompt — collaboration and resistance — which they haven't addressed yet. This will round out their reflection and connect all the threads.

exchanges.student: I think actors collaborate when they need each other’s resources, authority, or local knowledge, or when cooperation helps make a plan look more legitimate. Resistance probably happens when groups think planning threatens their interests, excludes them from decision-making, or shifts resources and control away from them. I feel a little fuzzy on how to explain resistance without making it sound automatically bad, though.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to question that framing. Think about it this way: what might resistance *reveal* about a planning process? Could it serve a function beyond just blocking something?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good intuitions about both collaboration and resistance, and has self-identified a nuance they're struggling with — how to frame resistance without making it sound inherently negative. This is a sophisticated conceptual issue worth exploring. Rather than explaining it for them, I should ask a question that helps them think about what resistance can accomplish or signal in a planning context.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think resistance can show that a planning process isn’t actually legitimate or inclusive, even if it claims to be. It might reveal unequal power, bad resource distribution, or that local people see the plan as imposed from above, so resistance can be a form of political feedback and not just obstruction.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely strong insight — resistance as a legitimacy signal is a more nuanced and defensible argument than simply calling it obstruction.

You now have material covering all four parts of the prompt. What do you think your central argument — the thread connecting power, legitimacy, the state, resources, collaboration, and resistance — actually is? Try to state it in one or two sentences.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a sophisticated and well-reasoned idea — resistance as political feedback and a legitimacy signal. This is strong material for their reflection. We've now covered all four parts of the prompt through discussion. Given the run configuration of 10 exchanges and we're at exchange 7, it's a good moment to push the student toward integrating everything into a written attempt, as the rules suggest when there's been sufficient conceptual development.

exchanges.student: Maybe my main argument is that multi-level planning is shaped by unequal power across scales, and legitimacy is what makes that power acceptable or contested. The state is both an actor and a site in this process, while control over resources determines who can actually implement plans, and collaboration or resistance shows whether planning arrangements are seen as legitimate.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid thesis — it's coherent and covers all parts of the prompt. Now it's time to stop planning and start writing. Take that argument and turn it into your opening paragraph. Come back with a draft when you have something down.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear and coherent central argument that ties all four parts of the prompt together. This is good progress. Since we've now built the conceptual foundation and they have a thesis, I should push them toward actually writing — not help them structure or polish the argument, but prompt them to start drafting. The rules say I should not write or suggest language for the student.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll try that. My opening paragraph will probably say that multi-level planning involves actors across local, national, and global scales, and that the key issue is not just coordination but who has power, who is seen as legitimate, and who controls resources.】【。json
exchanges.tutor: Looks like some stray characters snuck in there — no worries. But I notice you described your paragraph rather than actually writing it. Go ahead and write it out in full sentences and share it here — that's where the real work happens.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student seems to be starting to draft, which is good. However, they haven't actually shown me a written paragraph — they've described what it will say. I should push them to actually write the paragraph rather than summarize it. I also notice some odd characters at the end of their message (】【。json) which I should briefly acknowledge as likely a typo and refocus on the task.

exchanges.student:
Okay, here’s my try:

Multi-level planning involves the interaction of actors across local, regional, national, and international scales, but it is not simply a technical process of coordination. It is a political process shaped by unequal power, competing claims to legitimacy, and uneven control over resources. In this context, the state should be understood as both an actor that makes and enforces rules and a site where different interests, institutions, and external pressures meet. Because authority in multi-level planning is distributed unevenly, collaboration often depends on whether actors see planning arrangements as legitimate and beneficial, while resistance can reveal exclusion, conflict, or a lack of real participation.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely strong opening — your argument is clear and all four elements of the prompt are present. Good work getting it on paper.

Now keep going. What's the first idea from your thesis that you want to develop in the next paragraph?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a strong opening paragraph that integrates all four elements of the prompt into a coherent argument. It's clear, well-organized, and sets up the reflection well. I should give honest positive feedback without grading, and now push them to keep writing rather than dwelling on this paragraph. The conversation has been productive and we're near the end of the planned exchanges, so I should encourage momentum toward completing the full draft.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently asking questions that guided the student to generate their own ideas rather than providing answers. The tutor built progressively on each student response, scaffolded well from concept clarification to example to synthesis to drafting, and maintained a supportive, appropriately concise tone. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the student a conclusion, thesis, or submission-ready content — all key ideas (state as both site and actor, resistance as legitimacy signal, resource-authority gap) were generated by the student through guided questioning. 1.2: The tutor consistently acknowledged and built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming the decentralization example, the resistance insight) and used diagnostic questions before advancing. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, notably in Turn 9 where it redirected the student from describing a paragraph to actually writing it, demonstrating attention to method. 2.1 and 2.2: The conversation progressed without repetition or drift, staying tightly anchored to the assignment throughout. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were concise, well-paced, and consistently supportive without implying formal grading.
